hub_subtitle: Inglês executivo para entrevistas nos EUA e apresentações de alto impacto

lesson.menu_title: Used To and Would -- Telling the Story of 41 Years in Tech
lesson.menu_desc: Turn four decades of experience into narrative authority with used to and would -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 7 -- Used To and Would for Narrative Authority
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucia Nishiyama Serra | Lesson 7 -- Used To and Would for Narrative Authority

listenings.text: Let me tell you what this industry used to be like, because most people today have no idea. When I started, back in the mainframe era, we used to book computer time in advance. You would get a two-hour slot at three in the morning, and you would guard it like gold. There was no cloud, no laptops. A single machine used to fill an entire room. Every company used to run its own data center, and if a server failed, someone would drive in at midnight to fix it by hand. That whole landscape is obsolete now. In one decade, everything transformed. What used to take a team of thirty now takes one engineer and a single command. People ask me if I miss the old days. Honestly, no. But I would not trade the perspective. When you have watched the ground shift three times, you stop fearing the next change, and you start looking for it.

So they asked me how the job has changed since I started in nineteen eighty-three. Here is what I said. Back then, a senior technology leader used to be the person with all the answers. You would sit at the top, you would give the orders, and the team would execute. Technology used to move slowly, so a single decision could last for years. That era is gone. Today the landscape shifts every few months, and no leader can know everything. So the job has transformed. I used to manage systems; now I build trust. I used to protect the old way; now I help my team evolve with the change. When I reflect on forty-one years, that is the biggest shift of all. It is not in the technology, but in what leadership means. The best leaders today do not pretend to be certain. They stay curious, and they keep learning out loud.

extra_audio.text: When I started in the mainframe era, I used to manage everything by hand. Every night, I would stay late to check each server one by one. Then the shift to the cloud transformed the whole landscape. The systems I used to run are completely obsolete now. When I reflect on it, I evolved with every decade.
